answer:
**Iqbal's operative claims in "Wataniyat" (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 25):**

Iqbal portrays the modern Nation ("wataniyat") as the greatest idol forged by contemporary philosophy and civilization, which desecrates faith by appropriating religion's garb.[^1] He contrasts state discourse, where "Homeland" (*wataniyat*) is mere land, with the Prophet's teaching, where it is divinely commanded and universal.[^2] Territorial confinement is a calamity; true freedom for Muslims lies in the global ocean of Islam, echoing the Prophet's migratory way.[^3] Nationalism fosters rivalry among peoples, turns trade into conquest, corrupts politics with deceit, subjugates the weak, fragments God's creation, and severs Islam's brotherhood—urging its destruction as a hollow idol.[^4]

**Charles Comte's operative claims on national territory (Traité de la propriété Vol. I):**

Comte establishes national territory as the highest property after common things, naturally belonging to each people/nation as its basin defined by mountains/seas, essential for existence and enclosing all lesser properties (individual/communal/provincial); even egalitarians recognize it as indestructible.[^5] This territory originates in natural occupation by families/tribes/hordes since antiquity, guaranteed by necessity/defense rather than governments.[^6] Its conversion into private properties via cultivation advances society: savages needing vast uncultivated land for hunting yield to agriculturists who multiply subsistence/population/wealth on fractions thereof, abandoning "primitive" shares while enhancing communal value—thus no usurpation, but creation of security, industry, and progress from barbarism.[^7]

**Substantive disagreement vs. vocabulary difference:**

The core tension is substantive, not mere vocabulary. Iqbal rejects *territorial confinement as such*—even natural/divine—as idolatrous when elevated to "Nation," prioritizing Islamic universalism/brotherhood over bounded loyalty, which inevitably breeds rivalry/conquest.[^8] Comte *affirms* bounded territory (natural, not arbitrary) as foundational property enabling moral/civilizational progress: secure existence, labor division, population growth, wealth creation, and escape from savage misery—implicitly moral via liberty/property rights vs. violence/slavery.[^9]

Vocabulary partially diverges: Iqbal's "Nation" evokes 20th-century political nationalism (statist, rivalrous, faith-usurping idol); Comte's "nation/peuple" denotes pre-political natural grouping (tribal/ethnic basin-occupiers), akin to extended family property. Yet substantively, both address *territorial bounding*: Iqbal deems it calamitous/divisive; Comte, providential/progressive. No corpus evidence of Comte explicitly tying it to "moral progress," but his framework frames property-secured cultivation as civilizational ascent (physical/intellectual/moral implicitly). Iqbal's religious universalism clashes with Comte's naturalistic particularism; the corpus lacks direct reconciliation.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [ch. 25 "Wataniyat"](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-79b1e9).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [ch. 25 "Wataniyat"](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [ch. 25 "Wataniyat"](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-a723b2).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [ch. 25 "Wataniyat"](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/#p-97937b).

[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [ch. 7 "Du territoire propre à chaque nation"](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-2cfb18).

[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [ch. 7 "Du territoire propre à chaque nation"](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-982e4a); [ch. 8 "Des limites naturelles du territoire propre à chaque nation... "](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/08-des-limites-naturelles-du-territoire-propre-a-chaq/translation/#p-f47250).

[^7]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [ch. 10 "De la conversion du territoire national en propriétés privées"](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/10-de-la-conversion-du-territoire-national-en-proprie/translation/#p-30fbf4).

[^8]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [ch. 25 "Wataniyat"](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/25-wataniyat/translation/).

[^9]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [ch. 10 "De la conversion du territoire national en propriétés privées"](https://falsafa-corpus.com/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/10-de-la-conversion-du-territoire-national-en-proprie/translation/#p-944baa).
